questions.question: Some group (G, o) is known to be abelian. Then, which one of the following is true for G? A. g = g^(-1) for every g in G  B. g = g^2 for every g in G  C. (g o h)^2 = g^2 o h^2 for every g, h in G  D. G is of finite order
questions.answer: C. (g o h)^2 = g^2 o h^2 for every g, h in G
questions.to_find: The property that necessarily holds in every abelian group

understand.plain: A group is abelian when its operation is commutative, i.e. a o b = b o a for all elements. The trick here is to test which statement follows from commutativity alone, without any extra assumption. Options A, B and D each force a special structure (every element is its own inverse, every element is idempotent, or finiteness), but commutativity by itself only lets us reorder factors, which is exactly what turns (g o h)^2 into g^2 o h^2.

keywords.explain: A group in which the operation commutes: a o b = b o a for all a, b.
The freedom to swap the order of two operands without changing the result.
keywords.example: In an abelian group g o h o g o h can be rearranged to g o g o h o h.

keywords.explain: A single instance where a proposed universal statement fails, proving it false.
keywords.example: In (Z, +) the element 3 has 3 != -3, so 'g = g^(-1) for all g' is false.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='500' height='180' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='30' y='45'>(g o h)^2 = (g o h) o (g o h)</text><text class='sub mut' x='30' y='75'>= g o (h o g) o h    (associativity)</text><text class='sub ink' x='30' y='105'>h o g = g o h    (commutativity holds here)</text><text class='lbl ok' x='30' y='140'>= g o (g o h) o h = g^2 o h^2</text><text class='tag mut' x='30' y='172'>Only commutativity is needed - option C</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A short derivation showing that expanding (g o h)^2 and swapping the middle pair using commutativity yields g^2 o h^2.

given.aim: Identify which listed property must hold in any abelian group.

terms.meaning: The underlying set of the group, with operation o.
terms.example: G could be the integers under addition.
terms.connects: Every statement is quantified over elements g, h of G.

terms.meaning: The group operation, here assumed commutative.
terms.example: If o is +, then g o h means g + h.
terms.connects: Commutativity of o is the only extra fact we may use.

terms.meaning: The inverse of g, the element with g o g^(-1) = e.
terms.connects: Option A wrongly claims every element equals its own inverse.

given.plan: Expand (g o h)^2, use associativity to regroup and commutativity to swap the middle factors, and confirm the others fail by counterexample in (Z, +).

steps.title: Expand the square in option C
steps.formula_raw: (g o h)^2 = (g o h) o (g o h) = g o (h o g) o h
steps.apply: Associativity lets us drop brackets and isolate the middle pair h o g.
steps.note: No commutativity used yet, this holds in any group.

steps.title: Apply commutativity
steps.formula_raw: h o g = g o h
steps.apply: Replacing h o g by g o h gives g o (g o h) o h = (g o g) o (h o h) = g^2 o h^2.
steps.note: This is exactly the abelian hypothesis, so option C must be true.

steps.title: Reject A, B, D by counterexample
steps.apply: (Z, +) is abelian but fails A (g = g^(-1)), B (g = g^2) and D (finite order).
steps.note: A single abelian group violating each shows those statements are not forced.

solution.result: C. (g o h)^2 = g^2 o h^2 for every g, h in G

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that (g o h)^2 = g^2 o h^2 is logically equivalent to commutativity, so it holds in exactly the abelian groups.

questions.question: Let A be the set of all non-singular matrices over real numbers and let * be the matrix multiplication operation. Then A. A is closed under * but <A, *> is not a semigroup.  B. <A, *> is a semigroup but not a monoid.  C. <A, *> is a monoid but not a group.  D. <A, *> is a group but not an abelian group.
questions.answer: D. <A, *> is a group but not an abelian group
questions.to_find: The strongest correct algebraic classification of <A, *>

understand.plain: A is the set of all real invertible (non-singular) matrices of a fixed size, under matrix multiplication, forming the general linear group GL(n, R). The product of two invertible matrices is invertible (closure), multiplication is associative, the identity matrix I is the unit, and every non-singular matrix has an inverse, so it is a group. It is not abelian because matrix multiplication generally does not commute.

keywords.explain: A square matrix with nonzero determinant, hence invertible.
keywords.example: [[1,0],[0,2]] is non-singular since its determinant is 2.

keywords.explain: A set with an associative operation, an identity element, and inverses for every element.
keywords.example: GL(2, R), the 2x2 invertible real matrices, is a group under multiplication.

keywords.explain: An operation where order matters: AB need not equal BA.
keywords.example: Two rotation/shear matrices A, B usually satisfy AB != BA.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='520' height='190' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='42'>Group axioms for <A, *>:</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='72'>Closure: det(AB) = det(A)det(B) != 0</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='98'>Associative: A(BC) = (AB)C</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='124'>Identity: I (the unit matrix)</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='150'>Inverse: A^(-1) exists since det != 0</text><text class='sub no' x='28' y='182'>Commutative: AB != BA in general -> not abelian</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A checklist confirming closure, associativity, identity and inverses hold for non-singular matrices, while commutativity fails.

given.aim: Classify the structure of non-singular real matrices under multiplication.

terms.meaning: Set of all non-singular (invertible) real matrices of a fixed order n.
terms.example: For n = 2, A contains [[1,1],[0,1]] but not the zero matrix.
terms.connects: A with * is the general linear group GL(n, R).
It is associative but not commutative.

terms.meaning: The identity matrix, the unit for matrix multiplication.
terms.connects: Its existence makes the structure a monoid, then a group.

terms.meaning: The matrix inverse, which exists exactly when det(A) != 0.
terms.connects: Guaranteed inverses upgrade the monoid to a group.

given.plan: Check the four group axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse) for non-singular matrices, then test commutativity to decide abelian or not.

steps.title: Closure and associativity
steps.apply: Product of two non-singular matrices has nonzero determinant, so it stays in A; matrix multiplication is always associative.
steps.note: So <A, *> is at least a semigroup.

steps.title: Identity and inverses
steps.formula_raw: I in A;  A * A^(-1) = A^(-1) * A = I for every A in A
steps.apply: I is non-singular (det = 1), and every non-singular A has an inverse A^(-1) which is also non-singular.
steps.note: Identity makes it a monoid; inverses make it a group.

steps.title: Test commutativity
steps.apply: A concrete pair of invertible matrices fails to commute, so the group is non-abelian.
steps.note: Hence it is a group but not an abelian group.

solution.result: D. <A, *> is a group but not an abelian group

questions.formula_note: Non-singular matrices under multiplication form GL(n, R), a group; the failure of AB = BA in general rules out the abelian option.

questions.question: Let G1 and G2 be subgroups of a group G. A. Show that G1 intersect G2 is also a subgroup of G. B. Is G1 union G2 always a subgroup of G?
questions.answer: The intersection G1 ∩ G2 is always a subgroup of G; the union G1 ∪ G2 is a subgroup if and only if one of G1, G2 contains the other, so it is NOT always a subgroup.
questions.to_find: Whether intersection and union of two subgroups are again subgroups

understand.plain: The subgroup test says a non-empty subset H is a subgroup precisely when it is closed under the operation and under inverses (equivalently a * b^(-1) lies in H). The intersection of two subgroups inherits both properties, so it is always a subgroup. The union usually is not, because picking one element from each part can produce a product that lies in neither.

keywords.explain: A non-empty subset that is itself a group under the same operation.
keywords.example: The even integers 2Z are a subgroup of (Z, +).

keywords.explain: H (non-empty) is a subgroup iff for all a, b in H, a * b^(-1) is in H.
keywords.example: For 2Z: any difference of two even numbers is even.

keywords.explain: An element formed by combining members of a set must stay inside the set.
keywords.example: 2Z union 3Z is not closed: 2 + 3 = 5 lies in neither.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='500' height='210' rx='10'/><circle cx='200' cy='110' r='80' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='300' cy='110' r='80' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text class='lbl ink' x='150' y='60'>G1</text><text class='lbl ink' x='330' y='60'>G2</text><path d='M 250 45 A 80 80 0 0 1 250 175 A 80 80 0 0 1 250 45 Z' class='hi' opacity='0.6'/><text class='tag ok' x='232' y='115'>G1 cap G2</text><text class='sub ok' x='30' y='205'>Intersection: subgroup (always)</text><text class='sub no' x='300' y='205'>Union: not always closed</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram of two subgroups G1 and G2 with their overlapping intersection highlighted as the guaranteed subgroup, while the union is flagged as generally not closed.

given.aim: Prove G1 ∩ G2 is a subgroup and decide whether G1 ∪ G2 always is.

terms.meaning: The ambient group with operation * and identity e.
terms.connects: Both G1 and G2 sit inside G and share its operation.

terms.meaning: Two subgroups of G, each closed and containing inverses.
terms.connects: Their intersection and union are the subsets under study.

terms.meaning: The identity element, which lies in every subgroup.
terms.example: 0 is in both 2Z and 3Z, so 0 is in their intersection.
terms.connects: It guarantees G1 ∩ G2 is non-empty.

given.plan: Use the one-step subgroup test (a * b^(-1) in H) on the intersection, then exhibit a counterexample showing the union need not be closed.

steps.title: Intersection is non-empty
steps.formula_raw: e in G1 and e in G2  =>  e in G1 cap G2
steps.apply: Every subgroup contains the identity e, so e lies in both, hence in G1 ∩ G2.
steps.note: The intersection is non-empty, a prerequisite for the test.

steps.title: Apply the subgroup test to the intersection
steps.formula_raw: a, b in G1 cap G2  =>  a*b^(-1) in G1 and a*b^(-1) in G2  =>  a*b^(-1) in G1 cap G2
steps.apply: If a, b are in both subgroups then a * b^(-1) is in each (since each is a subgroup), so it is in the intersection.
steps.note: By the one-step test, G1 ∩ G2 is a subgroup of G.

steps.title: Counterexample for the union
steps.formula_raw: G = (Z,+), G1 = 2Z, G2 = 3Z;  2 in G1, 3 in G2 but 2+3 = 5 not in 2Z union 3Z
steps.apply: 5 is neither even nor a multiple of 3, so the union is not closed under +.
steps.note: Thus G1 ∪ G2 is not always a subgroup; it is one only when G1 contains G2 or vice versa.

solution.result: G1 ∩ G2 is always a subgroup of G; G1 ∪ G2 is a subgroup only when one contains the other, so it is not always a subgroup.

questions.formula_note: The one-step subgroup test (a * b^(-1) in H) settles both parts; the union fails because closure can break across the two pieces.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is FALSE? A. The set of rational numbers is an abelian group under addition.  B. The set of integers is an abelian group under addition.  C. The set of rational numbers forms an abelian group under multiplication.  D. The set of real numbers excluding zero is an abelian group under multiplication.
questions.answer: C. The set of rational numbers forms an abelian group under multiplication

understand.plain: For a set to be a group under an operation, every element must have an inverse. Under multiplication, the inverse of x is 1/x, which does not exist for 0. The full set of rationals Q includes 0, and 0 has no multiplicative inverse, so Q is not a group under multiplication. The other three sets satisfy all group axioms with their stated operations.

keywords.explain: For x, the element 1/x with x * (1/x) = 1; it fails to exist for x = 0.
keywords.example: The inverse of 5 is 1/5, but 0 has no inverse.

keywords.explain: A commutative group: closed, associative, with identity and inverses, and a*b = b*a.
keywords.example: (Q, +) is an abelian group with identity 0.

keywords.explain: Removing 0 from a number set so that every remaining element is invertible under multiplication.
keywords.example: R\{0} is a group under multiplication, unlike all of R.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='520' height='180' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='44'>(Q, +)</text><text class='sub ok' x='160' y='44'>identity 0, inverse -x  -> group OK</text><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='84'>(Z, +)</text><text class='sub ok' x='160' y='84'>identity 0, inverse -n  -> group OK</text><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='124'>(Q, *)</text><text class='sub no' x='160' y='124'>0 has no inverse 1/0  -> NOT a group</text><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='164'>(R\{0}, *)</text><text class='sub ok' x='160' y='164'>0 removed, inverse 1/x  -> group OK</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A comparison of the four candidate structures showing all are groups except (Q, *), which fails because 0 has no multiplicative inverse.

given.aim: Find the one statement among four that is not a valid group.

terms.meaning: The set of all rational numbers, including 0.
terms.connects: Under multiplication, the presence of 0 breaks the inverse axiom.

terms.meaning: The set of integers, forming an abelian group under addition.
terms.connects: Statement B is a textbook-true group.

terms.meaning: Real numbers with 0 removed, a group under multiplication.
terms.example: Inverse of 2 is 0.5; 0 is excluded so no problem.

given.plan: Check the multiplicative-inverse axiom for each set; the set that contains 0 under multiplication fails and is the false statement.

steps.title: Verify the additive groups
steps.formula_raw: identity 0, inverse of x is -x; addition is commutative and associative
steps.apply: (Q, +) and (Z, +) both have identity 0 and additive inverses, so A and B are true.
steps.note: Addition never has an inverse problem on number sets.

steps.title: Test (Q, *) for inverses
steps.formula_raw: for group under *, every x needs 1/x; but 0 in Q has no inverse
steps.apply: 0 is a rational number with no multiplicative inverse, so (Q, *) is not a group.

steps.title: Confirm (R\{0}, *) is fine
steps.formula_raw: identity 1, inverse of x is 1/x for x != 0; multiplication commutes
steps.apply: Removing 0 leaves every element invertible, so D is a true abelian group.
steps.note: Only C fails, isolating it as the false statement.

solution.result: C. The set of rational numbers forms an abelian group under multiplication

questions.formula_note: The decisive axiom is invertibility: 0 in Q has no multiplicative inverse, so (Q, *) is not a group, making statement C false.

questions.question: Which one of the following is false? A. The set of all bijective functions on a finite set forms a group under function composition.  B. The set {1, 2, ..., p-1} forms a group under multiplication mod p, where p is a prime number.  C. The set of all strings over a finite alphabet forms a group under concatenation.  D. A subset S != empty of G is a subgroup of the group <G, *> if and only if for any pair of elements a, b in S, a * b^(-1) in S.
questions.answer: C. The set of all strings over a finite alphabet forms a group under concatenation

understand.plain: Strings under concatenation are closed and associative and have an identity (the empty string), so they form a monoid; but no non-empty string has an inverse, since you cannot concatenate something to a string to get back the empty string. Hence concatenation gives a monoid, not a group, making C false. The other three are standard true facts: the symmetric group on a finite set, the multiplicative group of nonzero residues mod a prime, and the one-step subgroup test.

keywords.explain: A monoid has closure, associativity and identity; a group also requires inverses for every element.
keywords.example: Strings under concatenation are a monoid but not a group.

keywords.explain: All bijections (permutations) of a set under composition form a group, with identity the identity map.
keywords.example: The 6 permutations of {1,2,3} form S_3 under composition.

keywords.explain: {1, ..., p-1} under multiplication mod a prime p is a group; every element has an inverse because p is prime.
keywords.example: Mod 5: 2 * 3 = 6 = 1, so 3 is the inverse of 2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='520' height='180' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='44'>Strings under concatenation:</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='76'>Closure: ab . cd = abcd</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='102'>Associative; identity = empty string ""</text><text class='sub no' x='28' y='134'>Inverse of "ab"? no string x with ab.x = ""</text><text class='tag no' x='28' y='170'>=> monoid, NOT a group  (statement C is false)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An axiom check for strings under concatenation showing closure, associativity and an identity but no inverses, so it is a monoid not a group.

given.aim: Identify the single false claim about group structures.

terms.meaning: All finite sequences of symbols, combined by concatenation.
terms.connects: Lack of inverses makes this a monoid, breaking statement C.
It is the would-be group identity, but inverses still fail.

terms.meaning: Nonzero residues under multiplication modulo a prime p.
terms.connects: Primality guarantees inverses, making statement B true.

given.plan: Check each structure against the group axioms; the concatenation monoid lacks inverses, so the claim that it is a group is false.

steps.title: Validate A, B, D
steps.formula_raw: bijections -> symmetric group; {1..p-1} mod p -> every a has inverse since gcd(a,p)=1; a*b^(-1) in S is the subgroup test
steps.apply: Bijections on a finite set form a group under composition (A true); nonzero residues mod a prime form a multiplicative group (B true); the one-step subgroup test in D is a correct theorem.
steps.note: All three are standard true statements.

steps.title: Test strings under concatenation
steps.formula_raw: identity = empty string ''; but for non-empty w there is no x with w . x = ''
steps.apply: Concatenation is closed, associative and has the empty string as identity, but a non-empty string can never be shortened back to '' by concatenation, so inverses do not exist.
steps.note: It is a monoid, not a group, so statement C is false.

solution.result: C. The set of all strings over a finite alphabet forms a group under concatenation

questions.formula_note: Concatenation lacks inverses, so strings form a monoid rather than a group, making statement C the false one.

questions.question: Let (Z, *) be an algebraic structure where Z is the set of integers and the operation * is defined by n * m = max(n, m). Which of the following statements is true for (Z, *)? A. (Z, *) is a monoid.  B. (Z, *) is an Abelian group.  C. (Z, *) is a group.  D. None of the above.

understand.plain: The operation max is associative and commutative, so (Z, max) is at least a commutative semigroup. For a monoid we need an identity e with max(n, e) = n for all integers n; that would require e to be smaller than or equal to every integer, but Z has no smallest element, so no identity exists. Without an identity it cannot be a monoid, let alone a group, so the answer is None of the above.

keywords.explain: An element e with n * e = e * n = n for all n; for max it must satisfy max(n, e) = n.
keywords.example: max(n, e) = n forces e <= every integer, impossible in Z.

keywords.explain: A set with an associative operation, but not necessarily an identity or inverses.
keywords.example: (Z, max) is a commutative semigroup but not a monoid.

keywords.explain: An operation where x * x = x; max is idempotent, which blocks inverses.
keywords.example: max(3, 3) = 3, so 3 can never be 'undone' to reach an identity.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='520' height='180' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='28' y='44'>n * m = max(n, m) on Z</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='76'>Associative: max(max(a,b),c)=max(a,b,c)</text><text class='sub ok' x='28' y='102'>Commutative: max(n,m)=max(m,n)</text><text class='sub no' x='28' y='134'>Identity needs e <= every integer: none in Z</text><text class='tag no' x='28' y='170'>No identity -> not a monoid -> None of the above</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An axiom check for (Z, max) showing it is associative and commutative but has no identity element, so it is only a semigroup.

given.aim: Decide whether (Z, max) is a monoid, an abelian group, a group, or none.

terms.meaning: The set of all integers, unbounded below and above.
terms.connects: The lack of a minimum is exactly why no identity exists.

terms.meaning: n * m returns the larger of n and m.
terms.connects: max is associative and commutative but idempotent.

terms.meaning: A required element with max(n, e) = n for all n.
terms.example: Would need e <= n for every n, i.e. a smallest integer, which does not exist.
terms.connects: Its absence rules out monoid and group.

given.plan: Verify associativity and commutativity, then search for an identity element of max over Z; show none exists, which downgrades the structure to a mere semigroup.

steps.title: max is associative and commutative
steps.formula_raw: max(max(a,b),c) = max(a, max(b,c)) = max(a,b,c);  max(n,m) = max(m,n)
steps.apply: These identities hold for all integers, so (Z, max) is a commutative semigroup.
steps.note: Closure is automatic since max of two integers is an integer.

steps.title: Search for an identity
steps.formula_raw: need e with max(n, e) = n for all n  =>  e <= n for every integer n
steps.apply: No integer is less than or equal to every integer (Z has no minimum), so no identity element exists.
steps.note: Without an identity, (Z, max) is not a monoid.

steps.title: Eliminate group and abelian group
steps.formula_raw: no identity => no inverses => not a group => not an abelian group
steps.apply: Since even the monoid requirement fails, the group and abelian-group options fail too.
steps.note: Every option A, B, C is false, so the answer is D: None of the above.

questions.formula_note: max on Z is associative and commutative but has no identity (Z has no least element), so it is only a semigroup; hence none of monoid, group, or abelian group applies.

formulas.name: Group Axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse)
formulas.latex: \forall a,b,c\in G:\ a*b\in G,\ (a*b)*c=a*(b*c),\ \exists e:\ a*e=e*a=a,\ \exists a^{-1}:\ a*a^{-1}=e
formulas.plain: A set G with operation * is a group if it is closed, associative, has an identity e, and every element has an inverse; a monoid satisfies the first three, a semigroup the first two.
formulas.whenToUse: Use to classify an algebraic structure as semigroup, monoid, group, or abelian group by checking the axioms one at a time.

terms.means: the binary operation on the set
the identity element, satisfying a*e = e*a = a
the inverse of a, satisfying a*a^(-1) = e

formulas.trap: Forgetting that a monoid needs an identity and a group needs inverses; many structures (strings under concatenation, max on Z) satisfy only some axioms.
formulas.reference: Standard definition of group/monoid/semigroup in abstract algebra.

formulas.name: Abelian (Commutative) Group Condition
formulas.plain: A group is abelian when its operation commutes for every pair of elements.
formulas.whenToUse: Use to test whether a known group is commutative, or to deduce identities (such as (ab)^2 = a^2 b^2) that are equivalent to commutativity.

terms.means: any two elements of the group
the group operation that is being tested for commutativity

formulas.trap: Assuming all groups commute; matrix multiplication and function composition are common non-abelian examples.
formulas.reference: Definition of abelian group in group theory.

formulas.name: One-Step Subgroup Test
formulas.latex: H\neq\varnothing,\ H\subseteq G:\ H\le G \iff \forall a,b\in H,\ a*b^{-1}\in H
formulas.plain: A non-empty subset H of a group G is a subgroup if and only if for all a, b in H, the element a * b^(-1) is also in H.
formulas.whenToUse: Use to prove a subset is a subgroup, e.g. that the intersection of two subgroups is a subgroup.

terms.means: the inverse of b within the group G
the combined element that must stay inside H

formulas.trap: Skipping the non-empty check, or assuming a union of subgroups passes the test; it usually fails closure.
formulas.reference: One-step subgroup test, standard group theory result.

formulas.name: Square of a Product in an Abelian Group
formulas.plain: In a group, (a*b)^2 equals a^2 * b^2 exactly when a and b commute; thus this identity holds for all pairs precisely in an abelian group.
formulas.whenToUse: Use to recognize that the identity (gh)^2 = g^2 h^2 characterizes commutativity.
formulas.trap: Believing (ab)^2 = a^2 b^2 in every group; it requires commutativity of a and b.
formulas.reference: Consequence of the abelian group definition.
